Algeria.text: Algeria's group stage exit came with late-match theatre to spare: a pulsating 3-3 draw with Austria saw Mahrez equalise twice, the second deep into stoppage time, only for Kalajdzic to restore Austria's lead moments later in a breathless, chaotic finish that left Algeria scraping through as one of the best third-placed sides. Switzerland await in the Round of 32 on Thu Jul 2 20:00 PT — a genuinely open contest, with the Swiss a well-organised side but hardly imperious, and Algeria's attacking quality enough to make this a coin-flip. Survive that, and Colombia or Ghana could follow in the Round of 16, with the conceivable prospect of Argentina looming in the quarterfinals.

Argentina.text: Argentina swept through Group J without dropping a point, and their finale against Jordan was a controlled, professional performance — two goals before half-time, a brief wobble when Al-Tamari pulled one back on 55 minutes, then Messi settling matters with a tenth minute to play. Cape Verde await in the Round of 32 on Fri Jul 3 15:00 PT, a fixture the world champions will be expected to navigate without great difficulty, though the Islanders carry the unpredictability of a side with nothing to lose. Should Argentina advance through what looks a manageable Round of 16 against Australia or Egypt, a quarterfinal collision with Colombia or Switzerland could provide the tournament's first genuine test of their credentials.

Australia.text: Australia edged through Group D in second place on the back of a goalless draw with Paraguay — a cautious, functional conclusion to the group stage that kept things tidy without illuminating much. They meet Egypt in the Round of 32 on Fri Jul 3 11:00 PT, a tie that represents a genuine opportunity: Australia carry modest but real ambitions into it, and Egypt, for all their African pedigree, are not a side expected to stop a well-organised Socceroos outfit. Survive that, and the Round of 16 on Tue Jul 7 09:00 PT could bring Argentina — an altogether different conversation, and one that would define the tournament's stakes in an instant.

Austria.text: Austria scraped through Group J in the most dramatic fashion: a 3-3 draw with Algeria that swung violently in both directions, Kalajdzic's injury-time equaliser rescuing a point after Mahrez had twice overturned Austrian leads, most damagingly with a goal at 90+3'. The final scoreline flatters no one, and the quality of the defending will give Austria's coaching staff considerable cause for concern. Spain await in the Round of 32 on Thu Jul 2 12:00 PT — a formidable assignment by any measure, with the Spaniards among the tournament's genuine contenders and Austria entering the tie as clear underdogs after a group stage that exposed their defensive fragility. Survive that, and either Croatia or Portugal could stand in the way in the Round of 16 — the kind of bracket that offers no sanctuary whatsoever.

Belgium.text: Belgium closed the group stage in style, dismantling New Zealand 5-1 in a performance of growing authority — Trossard's brace set the tone early, and the goals kept arriving with something close to ease, a brief consolation at 84 minutes doing nothing to spoil the afternoon. They meet Senegal in the Round of 32 on Wed Jul 1 13:00 PT, a tie Belgium enter as clear favourites, though the Africans carry genuine physical menace and will not fold quietly. Survive that, and Portugal or Spain could await in the quarterfinal — the point at which this Golden Generation's latest attempt at something lasting would face its sternest examination.

Bosnia & Herzegovina.text: Bosnia & Herzegovina closed the group stage with purpose, beating Qatar 3-1 in a performance that was largely comfortable after two quick goals before the half-hour mark put them in control — Qatar's reply just before the interval proving only a brief disruption before Mahmic sealed it late. They advance as one of the better third-placed finishers, though the reward is a Round of 32 date with host nation USA on Wed Jul 1 17:00 PT, a daunting assignment against a side carrying significant home-tournament momentum and crowd support. Survive that, and Belgium or Senegal await in the Round of 16 — with the prospect of Portugal or Spain looming in the quarterfinals beyond.

Brazil.text: Brazil edged past Japan in a Round of 32 tie that refused to be straightforward — Sano's 29th-minute opener forced a response, Casemiro delivered it midway through the second half, but the Selecão needed a Martinelli strike deep in stoppage time to put the match to bed. Norway await in the Round of 16 on Sun Jul 5 13:00 PT, a well-organised European side capable of making life uncomfortable, though Brazil, as five-time world champions and group winners, enter as clear favourites. Should they advance, England or Argentina lurk as entirely plausible opponents further down the draw.

Canada.text: Canada edged past South Africa with the narrowest of margins, Stephen Eustáquio's stoppage-time goal the only thing separating the sides in a match that offered little comfort until the very last breath. Morocco await in the Round of 16 on Sat Jul 4 10:00 PT — an Atlas Lions side of genuine continental pedigree and tactical discipline, making this a real test for a Canadian team that has yet to convince in open play. Survive that, and either France or Paraguay could stand between Canada and the last eight, with the semifinal potentially throwing up the likes of Spain, Portugal, or the host nation USA.

Cape Verde.text: Cape Verde ground out three draws across the group stage without conceding, the goalless stalemate with Saudi Arabia a fitting emblem of a campaign built on defensive resolve rather than attacking ambition. That solidity earned them second place in Group H, but it also raises pointed questions about their capacity to hurt anyone come the knockouts. Argentina await in the Round of 32 on Fri Jul 3 15:00 PT — a formidable test by any measure, with the world champions heavily favoured and unlikely to be troubled by a side that has yet to score. Should Cape Verde somehow produce the upset of the tournament, Australia or Egypt would stand in their way in the Round of 16, with Colombia or Switzerland among the possible quarterfinal opponents beyond that.

Colombia.text: Colombia close out Group K with a disciplined goalless draw against Portugal — a result that tells its own story: this is a side composed enough to absorb a European heavyweight and come away unscathed, topping their group in the process. Ghana await in the Round of 32 on Fri Jul 3 18:30 PT, a tie Colombia enter as clear favorites, with the West Africans unlikely to trouble a team that has shown genuine defensive solidity and attacking ambition throughout. Win that, and either Algeria or Switzerland surface in the Round of 16, before the draw opens toward a potential quarterfinal collision with Argentina — a rivalry that would announce Colombia's tournament in the loudest possible terms.

Croatia.text: Croatia edged past Ghana 2-1 in their final group match, Sucic's first-half strike giving them control before Luckassen's equaliser threatened to derail proceedings — Vlasic's late winner restoring order and securing second place in Group L. Now comes a considerably stiffer examination: Portugal await in the Round of 32 on Thu Jul 2 16:00 PT, a tie in which Zlatko Dalic's side enter as underdogs against one of Europe's most formidable squads, though Croatia's tournament pedigree makes them dangerous rather than merely hopeful. Survive that, and Spain or Austria could follow in the Round of 16, with France or Morocco lurking as potential semifinal opponents.

Curaçao.text: Curaçao's group stage ended with a 0-2 defeat to Ivory Coast, Nicolas Pépé doing the damage with goals either side of half-time — the first arriving inside seven minutes to set a tone the Curaçao defence never recovered from. They finished fourth in Group E with a single point and a goal difference of minus nine, yet still advanced to the Round of 32 as one of the tournament's best third-placed sides. There, their journey came to an abrupt end, the gulf in quality across the group stage ultimately a true reflection of where this Curaçao side stands on the world stage.

Czech Republic.text: Czech Republic's group stage ended with a thumping 3-0 defeat to Mexico, the goals arriving in a clinical second-half burst and a stoppage-time finish that underlined the gulf in class — a deflating exit for a side that managed just one point across three matches. Having finished fourth in Group A and crept through as one of the best third-placed teams, they were nonetheless unable to sustain that precarious foothold, and Mexico's composed, purposeful display exposed their limitations emphatically. The Czechs depart at the earliest opportunity, their tournament leaving little to build on.

DR Congo.text: DR Congo closed the group stage with a performance that gathered momentum as it went: Uzbekistan led through Shomurodov's tenth-minute strike, but two penalties and a brace from Wissa, plus Mayele's finish, produced a 3-1 win that felt increasingly comfortable once the Leopards found their rhythm in the final quarter-hour. They now face England in the Round of 32 on Wed Jul 1 09:00 PT — as daunting a draw as any third-placed side could conjure, with Gareth Southgate's established tournament machine heavy favourites against a team that only just squeezed through. Survive that, and Mexico await in the Round of 16; beyond them, Brazil or Norway could loom in the quarterfinals — a bracket that offers no gentle passages, only the chance to build something remarkable against the odds.

Ecuador.text: Ecuador's exit arrived without much ceremony — a 0-2 defeat to Mexico in the Round of 32 in which they never truly threatened, conceding twice before half-time through Quiñones and a Jiménez strike deep in first-half stoppage time. The pattern was emphatic: Ecuador chasing the game from the half-hour mark, unable to find a response across 90 minutes. They had navigated the group stage only as one of the better third-placed sides, and against a Mexican outfit with genuine tournament ambition on home soil, that fragile foundation gave way entirely.

Egypt.text: Egypt finished their group campaign with a draw that flattered neither side — Saber's fifth-minute opener was cancelled out by Rezaeian just nine minutes later, and the 1-1 scoreline against Iran had the feel of two teams content to bank a point. They face Australia in the Round of 32 on Fri Jul 3 11:00 PT, a fixture Egypt will approach as moderate favorites, though the Socceroos are no mere formality and will press for an upset in front of what promises to be a combustible atmosphere. Survive that, and Argentina could await in the Round of 16 — a collision that would represent a genuine examination of just how far Mohamed Salah's generation can carry this side.

England.text: England closed out Group L with a controlled 2-0 win over Panama, Bellingham and Kane striking within five minutes of each other midway through the second half to settle matters without alarm. DR Congo await in the Round of 32 on Wed Jul 1 09:00 PT, a tie England enter as heavy favourites, though the Congolese have shown enough at this tournament to demand respect rather than complacency. Survive that, and a Round of 16 appointment with Mexico on Sun Jul 5 17:00 PT offers a sharper test — a proud footballing nation on home soil that will fancy their chances. Should England come through, Brazil or Norway could stand between them and the final four on Sat Jul 11 14:00 PT, with France, Spain, or Portugal potentially lurking beyond that as the prize and the ultimate reckoning.

France.text: France dismantled Sweden 3-0 in the Round of 32 with a performance that left little room for debate — Mbappé bookended the scoring either side of Barcola's strike, the tie effectively settled before the hour. Paraguay await in the Round of 16 on Sat Jul 4 14:00 PT, a fixture that reads as a manageable assignment for a side of France's quality, though the South Americans' physical resilience will demand concentration. Should Les Bleus advance, Canada or Morocco loom in the quarterfinal, with Portugal, Spain, or the United States among the heavyweights capable of mounting a genuine challenge in the semifinal.

Germany.text: Germany's Round of 32 exit is as brutal as the format can produce: Havertz's second-half equaliser cancelled out Enciso's opener, and when neither side could be separated across 90 minutes, Paraguay held their nerve in the shootout, converting four of four while Germany stumbled to three. A group-stage winner undone before the last sixteen, Joachim Löw's side — or whoever currently stewards this generation — departs a tournament they entered with genuine ambitions, beaten not by a superior team across the pitch but by the cold lottery of penalties.

Ghana.text: Ghana limped into the knockout rounds on the back of a group-stage defeat to Croatia — Luckassen's 73rd-minute equaliser briefly threatened a point, but Vlasic settled it for the Croatians ten minutes from time, leaving the Black Stars to advance as one of the best third-placed sides rather than group winners. Colombia await in the Round of 32 on Fri Jul 3 18:30 PT, a fixture that represents a stern examination: the South Americans carry greater pedigree and will be favoured, though Ghana's survival instinct — demonstrated across a scrappy, uneven group campaign — makes them a nuisance rather than a pushover. Survive that, and Algeria or Switzerland could follow before the possibility of Argentina looms in the quarterfinal.

Haiti.text: Haiti depart the 2026 World Cup without a point to their name, their group stage exit confirmed by a 4-2 defeat to Morocco that was comfortable for the Atlas Lions despite a spirited Haitian showing either side of half-time. Two goals inside the first 43 minutes gave Haiti brief cause for optimism, but Morocco reasserted themselves emphatically in the closing stages, scoring four times in total to send their opponents home. The Caribbeans finish bottom of Group C, undone by a gulf in quality that proved too wide to bridge across three matches.

Iran.text: Iran's group-stage finale against Egypt ended 1-1, a Rezaeian equaliser on 14 minutes cancelling out Saber's early opener — a pattern that defined their entire tournament: always in the contest, never quite through it. Three draws, no wins, and three points were enough to sneak through as one of the best third-placed sides, but the knockout round delivered the reckoning that the group stage had deferred. Iran exit the 2026 World Cup without a victory to their name, their campaign a study in stubborn resilience that ultimately fell short of the cutting edge a deep run demands.

Iraq.text: Iraq's group stage ended in humiliation against Senegal, a 5-0 defeat that was effectively settled inside the opening minutes and then ruthlessly extended across the second half, with Gueye alone scoring twice in a 12-minute spell. Finishing fourth in Group I with three losses, no points, and twelve goals conceded, Iraq's tournament was over at the earliest stage — a chastening introduction to a World Cup where the gulf in class between the continent's elite and the tournament's newcomers was laid painfully bare.

Ivory Coast.text: Ivory Coast gave Norway a genuine contest in the Round of 32, pulling level through Diallo on 74 minutes after falling behind to Nusa's first-half opener — only for Haaland to settle it with four minutes remaining, a late hammer blow that extinguished the comeback. The Elephants depart a tournament they had navigated with enough promise to suggest they belonged, yet ultimately could not withstand Norway's cutting edge when it mattered most.

Japan.text: Japan held their own against Brazil for the better part of an hour, Sano's 29th-minute opener giving them genuine belief before Casemiro levelled midway through the second half. It was Martinelli's stoppage-time winner that delivered the cruellest of exits — a Samurai Blue side that had navigated Group F without defeat ultimately undone in the final seconds of a Round of 32 tie they had come agonisingly close to taking further.

Jordan.text: Jordan's group stage ended with a 3-1 defeat to Argentina — a result that followed the script almost from the first whistle, with the South Americans establishing a two-goal cushion inside half an hour before Messi put the contest beyond doubt late on. Al-Tamari's consolation just after the hour offered a brief flicker, but Jordan were outclassed. Finishing fourth in Group J with three defeats and no points, they nevertheless advanced as one of the tournament's best third-placed sides before their World Cup came to a close.

Mexico.text: Mexico swept through the group stage with a perfect record and kept that momentum rolling against Ecuador, two first-half goals — the second arriving deep in stoppage time — making for a composed, professional win that never looked in doubt. They meet either DR Congo or England in the Round of 16 on Sun Jul 5 17:00 PT: DR Congo would represent a manageable assignment, but England carry genuine threat and would constitute a proper examination of Mexico's ambitions. Win that, and Brazil or Norway could await in the quarterfinal — the prospect of facing Brazil on this stage, should it materialise, would be the tournament's first real reckoning for El Tri.

Morocco.text: Morocco's Round of 32 exit from Group C runners-up spot produced one of the tournament's most dramatic nights: Cyle Gakpo put the Netherlands ahead on 72 minutes, and Morocco looked bound for elimination before Diop's stoppage-time equaliser forced penalties, where the Atlas Lions held their nerve to win 3-2. Canada await in the Round of 16 on Sat Jul 4 10:00 PT — a more forgiving draw than Morocco might have feared, with the hosts still finding their footing at this level and Walid Regragui's side arriving on a wave of momentum. Should they advance, France or Paraguay loom in the quarterfinal, with a potential semifinal path that includes Spain and Portugal among the heavyweights still standing.

Netherlands.text: Netherlands dominated their group with seven points and a healthy goal difference, only to suffer one of the tournament's crueller exits — Gakpo's 72nd-minute strike cancelled out by a Moroccan equaliser deep in stoppage time, before the Dutch lost the subsequent shootout 2-3. It was the worst kind of departure: a lead surrendered at the death, then the lottery of penalties delivering the final blow. A side that had looked like genuine contenders through the group stage goes home from the Round of 32, undone in the space of twenty minutes.

New Zealand.text: Belgium administered a thorough lesson in the final group game, with Trossard's brace and further goals from De Bruyne and Lukaku turning what might have been a contest into a rout — New Zealand's consolation arriving far too late, in the 84th minute, to suggest any real foothold. The All Whites finished fourth in Group G with a single point and a goals difference of minus ten, their tournament record a frank reflection of the gulf between them and the competition's established forces. They depart a World Cup that offered little comfort beyond the experience itself.

Norway.text: Norway edged past Ivory Coast in a nervy Round of 32 encounter, Nusa's first-half strike giving them control before Diallo's equaliser with sixteen minutes left forced a tense finale — Haaland, inevitably, settled it four minutes from time. Now comes a considerably sterner examination: Brazil await in the Round of 16 on Sun Jul 5 13:00 PT, a fixture that reads, on reputation alone, as a heavy assignment for Ståle Solbakken's side, though Haaland's late-game menace ensures Norway are nobody's straightforward afternoon. Survive that, and England or Mexico could loom in the quarterfinal, with Argentina among the formidable possibilities should Norway somehow reach the last four.

Panama.text: Panama's group stage exit was as comprehensive as the record suggests — three defeats, no goals scored, and a closing loss to England that was settled efficiently in a five-minute spell midway through the second half, Bellingham and Kane putting the game to bed without drama or late anxiety. They finish bottom of Group L with nothing to show from their three outings, and whatever hope they carried into the tournament evaporated long before the knockout rounds began. A familiar story for Central American football at this level: the gap between qualifying and competing proved too wide to bridge.

Paraguay.text: Paraguay squeezed past Germany in the Round of 32 in the most nerve-shredding fashion — level at 1-1 after 90 minutes, Enciso's first-half strike cancelled out by Havertz shortly after the break, before the Guaraní held their nerve in a 4-3 shootout. That result alone buys them enormous credibility, but the reward is an immediate collision with France in the Round of 16 on Sat Jul 4 14:00 PT — a tie in which Les Bleus are heavy favourites and Paraguay must produce something close to a tournament-defining performance to survive. Win that, and Canada or Morocco await in the quarterfinal on Thu Jul 9 13:00 PT, a fixture that would offer genuine hope of a semifinal place — where Spain, Portugal, or Brazil could conceivably be waiting.

Portugal.text: Portugal closed their group stage with a goalless draw against Colombia — disciplined if uninspiring, the kind of match a side plays when a point is enough, the attacking impulse carefully rationed. They finished second in Group K and now face Croatia in the Round of 32 on Thu Jul 2 16:00 PT, a tie with genuine edge: these are two sides who know each other's rhythms well, and Croatia's tournament pedigree makes this far from comfortable, even for a Portuguese squad of considerable quality. Win that, and either Austria or the considerably stiffer challenge of Spain awaits in the Round of 16 — with France potentially looming as a heavyweight collision somewhere in the semifinal.

Qatar.text: Qatar's group stage exit arrived with little ceremony — a 1-3 defeat to Bosnia & Herzegovina in which they trailed by two goals before half-time, Al-Haydos pulling one back on the stroke of the interval before Mahmic's 80th-minute strike killed any lingering hope of a comeback. The hosts finished bottom of Group B with a single point and a goal difference of minus eight, their tournament a sobering exercise in the gap between ambition and execution at this level.

Saudi Arabia.text: Saudi Arabia's group stage ended with a goalless draw against Cape Verde — a stalemate that was as flat as the scoreline suggests, neither side able to manufacture a breakthrough across ninety minutes. That result, combined with a goal difference of minus five across three matches, proved just enough to squeeze through as one of the best third-placed teams, but the Green Falcons were eliminated in the Round of 32, their tournament ending as quietly as it had begun.

Scotland.text: Brazil ended Scotland's tournament before it had truly begun, a 3-0 defeat that was every bit as one-sided as the scoreline suggests. Júnior struck twice in the first half — the second arriving deep in added time — before Cunha added a third shortly after the interval, leaving Scotland with nothing to build on. They depart having advanced from the group stage as one of the best third-placed sides, a modest distinction that flatters a campaign yielding just one win and four goals conceded. The 2026 World Cup ends for Scotland at the Round of 32.

Senegal.text: Senegal closed the group stage with a emphatic 5-0 demolition of Iraq — Gueye's double the centrepiece of a second-half blitz that papered over what had been a turbulent campaign — to sneak through as one of the best third-placed sides. They now face Belgium in the Round of 32 on Wed Jul 1 13:00 PT, a genuinely daunting assignment: the Belgians carry the weight of a golden generation's unfinished business and arrive as clear favourites, leaving Senegal to rely on the kind of collective energy that overwhelmed Iraq. Survive that, and Portugal or Spain could be waiting in the quarterfinal.

South Africa.text: South Africa's World Cup ended in the cruelest fashion, a 90th-minute goal from Eustáquio giving Canada a 1-0 win that the balance of the match may not have fully demanded. Bafana Bafana had navigated the group stage with enough grit to finish second in Group A, but a tournament played on home soil — or at least in their confederate hemisphere — ultimately closed in the dying seconds of a Round of 32 tie they very nearly survived.

South Korea.text: South Korea close their World Cup 2026 account with a flat 0-1 defeat to South Africa, Maseko's goal just past the hour mark enough to settle a match the Taeguk Warriors never truly threatened to turn. They had squeezed through the group phase as one of the best third-placed sides, but that slender lifeline ran out in the round of 32 — eliminated without a goal scored in their final two outings, their tournament ending as quietly as it had begun.

Spain.text: Spain closed out Group H with a disciplined 1-0 win over Uruguay, Alex Baena's first-half strike doing just enough to keep a clean sheet intact and confirm top spot — a controlled, professional performance rather than a spectacular one. Austria await in the Round of 32 on Thu Jul 2 12:00 PT, a tie Spain enter as firm favourites; the Austrians are an organised, technically capable side but lack the pedigree to trouble a team of Spain's calibre across 90 minutes. Should they advance, a Round of 16 collision with either Croatia or Portugal looms as the tournament's first genuine examination — two nations with the quality and historical familiarity to make Spain work hard. France or Morocco could then be waiting in the semifinals, either capable of turning this into a serious reckoning.

Sweden.text: Sweden's tournament ends with a 0-3 defeat to France in the Round of 32 — a verdict that was effectively delivered at half-time, Mbappé's first strike just before the break puncturing whatever hope the Swedes had carried into the tie, and a Barcola goal eight minutes into the second half rendering the contest settled long before his second. They had advanced from Group F as one of the better third-placed sides, a narrow path that always pointed toward exactly this kind of reckoning: a French side operating at a different altitude. The margin was honest.

Switzerland.text: Switzerland closed out the group stage with a controlled 2-1 win over Canada, taking charge after the break through Vargas and Manzambi before absorbing a late Canadian consolation — a composed, professional finish to a seven-point campaign that secured top spot in Group B. Algeria await in the Round of 32 on Thu Jul 2 20:00 PT, a tie Switzerland enter as clear favourites: the Swiss have the organisation and the tournament pedigree to make this a routine assignment, though Algerian sides can be dangerous in transition. Should they advance through the last sixteen — where Colombia or Ghana lie in wait — a quarterfinal collision with Argentina looms as the credible examination of just how far this Switzerland side can go.

Tunisia.text: Tunisia's group stage exit was written early: two Dutch goals inside seven minutes buried the Carthage Eagles before the match had found its shape, and though Mastouri pulled one back after the break, Hecke's strike shortly after the hour restored the three-goal cushion and sealed a 3-1 defeat. Tunisia finished bottom of Group F without a point, their record of three defeats and a goal difference of minus ten telling the story of a tournament in which they were consistently outclassed — a dispiriting campaign ended against one of the competition's heavyweights.

Turkey.text: Turkey's tournament ends in the cruelest of fashions — a 3-2 win over the United States that arrived too late to matter. They trailed inside three minutes, fought back to lead through Güler and Yilmaz, conceded again early in the second half, and only sealed it through Ayhan's injury-time strike. The drama was real, the effort undeniable, but a fourth-place group finish — even as one of the better third-placed sides — proved an insufficient foundation, and Turkey's World Cup 2026 is over at the group stage.

USA.text: The USA closed the group stage with a bruising 3-2 defeat to Turkey — Trusty's third-minute opener briefly promised something bright, but Güler and Yilmaz had Turkey in command before the break, and an agonizing stoppage-time strike from Ayhan compounded a difficult evening. The hosts advance as Group D winners nonetheless, and face Bosnia & Herzegovina in the Round of 32 on Wed Jul 1 17:00 PT — a fixture the Americans enter as clear favorites, though Bosnia have shown enough tournament temperament to punish any complacency. Survive that, and a potential Round of 16 collision with Belgium or Senegal raises the stakes considerably, with a quarterfinal against the likes of Portugal or Spain looming as the real examination of how far this tournament can carry them.

Uruguay.text: Uruguay's group stage ended with a narrow 0-1 defeat to Spain, Álex Baena's first-half strike the solitary difference in a contest that offered the Uruguayans little joy in the final third. Despite advancing as one of the best third-placed sides, their tournament came to an abrupt halt at the same juncture — two draws and a loss across Group H, with a negative goal difference, telling a story of a side that never quite found its footing at this World Cup.

Uzbekistan.text: Uzbekistan's group stage ended with a 1-3 defeat to DR Congo, a result that traced a familiar arc: an encouraging early lead through Shomurodov, then a second-half capitulation as Wissa and Mayele dismantled them after the hour mark, Wissa adding a third in stoppage time to put a harsh gloss on the scoreline. The Central Asians finished bottom of Group K without a point, conceding eleven goals across three matches, and no late reprieve arrived. Their debut on the expanded World Cup stage was brief and bruising, a tournament that exposed the gulf between ambition and readiness at the highest level.
